title: Sharpening a sloyd knife
subtitle: The strop, the waterstone, and the edge that takes a hair off your forearm
excerpt: A sharp sloyd knife is the prerequisite for every other skill in wood carving. This guide covers the complete sharpening progression: the stropping cadence to maintain an edge between sessions, the waterstone sequence to rebuild one, and the oilstone for monthly maintenance. Includes the forearm-hair test.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. I (1882) for edge geometry and the progression from coarse stone to strop. Newey & Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1898) for bevel angle guidance.

content.text: A
sloyd knife

that cannot take a hair off the back of your forearm without pressing is not sharp enough to carve wood cleanly. A dull edge does not just produce a poor surface finish. It requires more force for every cut, which means less control, more slipping, and a much higher chance of the blade going somewhere other than where you intended it. Sharpening is not an optional extra in wood carving; it is the first technique to learn and the one to return to every fifteen minutes of carving time.
Understanding the edge
A standard sloyd knife has a scandi grind: a single flat
bevel

that runs from the spine of the blade down to the edge, meeting the flat back of the blade at the cutting edge itself. The angle is typically 12 to 16 degrees per side, measured from the flat of the blade. This single-bevel geometry is what makes a scandi knife easy to sharpen in the field: lay the bevel flat on the stone and it automatically registers the correct angle. No jig, no guesswork.

The enemy of the edge is the wire: a microscopic curl of metal that the stone raises on the back of the edge as it cuts. If you can feel a slightly rough, sharp catch when you draw your fingertip very lightly from the flat back of the blade across the edge, that is the wire. Stropping removes the wire and polishes the edge. Failing to strop after stoning leaves the wire in place and the edge feels dull despite being freshly stoned.
The stropping cadence
The
strop

is the maintenance tool. Strop every fifteen minutes of active carving, or whenever cuts begin to feel as though they are compressing rather than cutting the wood cleanly. For a mounted paddle strop, lay the
bevel

flat on the leather and draw the blade edge-trailing (spine first) along the strop from handle to tip. Six to eight strokes per side, alternating. The leather and abrasive compound do the work; no pressure is needed beyond keeping the bevel flat on the surface. Finish with two or three strokes on the flat back of the blade to align the edge.

A strop charged with green compound (chromium oxide) is the standard. Load the leather lightly and re-charge every few sessions. A strop that is loading up with grey metal residue needs a wipe with a dry cloth and a fresh charge of compound.
Rebuilding the edge on a waterstone

When stropping no longer restores the cutting feel, the edge needs a stone. This happens every few weeks of regular carving, or sooner if the knife has been used on very hard or abrasive wood, or if the tip has been chipped. The waterstone progression for a sloyd knife is: 1,000 grit to rebuild the bevel, 4,000 grit to refine it, then strop to finish.
Preparing the stone

Soak the 1,000-grit stone in clean water for five to ten minutes before use. The pores absorb water and the slurry that forms on the surface does the cutting. The 4,000-grit stone needs only a brief splash of water; it works better with a damp surface than a flooded one. Set both stones on a damp cloth that stops them sliding.
Sharpening on the 1,000-grit stone

Place the bevel flat on the 1,000-grit stone. The full length of the bevel should contact the stone, not just the tip or the heel. Draw the blade edge-leading away from you in a long stroke from heel to tip, using light, even pressure. The stroke should cover the full length of the stone for each pass. Ten to fifteen strokes per side, checking after each set of five by running a fingertip from the flat back across the edge. When a wire forms consistently along the full length of the edge, the 1,000 grit has done its work.
Refining on the 4,000-grit stone

Move to the 4,000-grit stone and repeat: bevel flat, edge-leading strokes, eight to ten per side. The slurry on the 4,000 grit is finer and the stone cuts less aggressively. The wire formed by the 1,000 grit will transfer and refine. Finish with three or four very light strokes per side with almost no pressure; these are polishing strokes, not material-removal strokes.
Strop to finish

Strop the edge as described above, ten strokes per side. The wire left by the stone will come away on the first few strokes. Finish on the flat back of the blade. Now test the edge on your forearm: hold the blade spine-down and draw it very lightly at a flat angle across the back of the forearm. If it takes a clean shave of hair without pressing, the edge is ready. If it merely bends the hairs, return to the strop for another round.
Oilstone maintenance

Once a month, or when the waterstone starts to dish (hollow in the middle), flatten it. Rub the stone face-down on 220-grit wet-and-dry paper taped to a flat surface. An India combination oilstone is a slower option that needs no soaking. It uses light machine oil as the lubricant. The method is the same. The India stone is less aggressive than the 1,000-grit waterstone, so it takes longer to raise a wire on a very dull edge.

attrs.intro: What goes wrong during sharpening and how to fix it.

items.fix: Slow the stroke and watch that the bevel contacts the stone from heel to tip throughout. Use a longer stroke that covers the full stone length on each pass.
items.cause: The stroke is not covering the full length of the bevel on each pass. The pressure or contact is concentrated in the centre of the stroke.
items.symptom: The edge is sharp in the middle but blunt toward the tip or heel

items.fix: Strop firmly for another ten strokes per side, finishing on the flat back. If the strop leather looks shiny and grey rather than slightly green, it needs fresh compound.
items.cause: The wire was not fully removed by the strop, or the strop compound is exhausted.
items.symptom: After stoning, the edge still fails the forearm-hair test

items.fix: Add a very slight secondary micro-bevel: five strokes with the blade lifted by one or two degrees from flat. This creates a tiny support behind the cutting edge that resists chipping.
items.cause: The bevel angle is too acute for the knife's steel or for the wood being carved. Hardwood end grain and dense dried timber chip acute edges.
items.symptom: Sharpening removes metal quickly but the edge still chips in use

attrs.heading: Common problems
